Abstract 
32 
Ras has traditionally been regarded as a positive regulator and therapeutic target due to 
33 
its role in cell proliferation, but recent findings indicate a more nuanced role in cell 
34 
migration, where suppressed Ras activity can unexpectedly promote migration. To 
35 
clarify this complexity, we systematically modulate Ras activity using various RasGEF 
36 
and RasGAP proteins and assess their effects on migration dynamics. Leveraging 
37 
optogenetics, we assess the immediate, non-transcriptional effects of Ras signaling on 
38 
migration. Local RasGEF recruitment to the plasma membrane induces protrusions and 
39 
new fronts to effectively guide migration, even in the absence of GPCR/G-protein 
40 
signaling whereas global recruitment causes immediate cell spreading halting cell 
41 
migration. Local RasGAP recruitment suppresses protrusions, generates new backs, 
42 
and repels cells whereas global relocation either eliminates all protrusions to inhibit 
43 
migration or preserves a single protrusion to maintain polarity.  Consistent local and 
44 
global increases or decreases in signal transduction and cytoskeletal activities 
45 
accompany these morphological changes. Additionally, we performed cortical tension 
46 
measurements and found that RasGEFs generally increase cortical tension while 
47 
RasGAPs decrease it. Our results reveal a biphasic relationship between Ras activity 
48 
and cellular dynamics, reinforcing our previous findings that optimal Ras activity and 
49 
cortical tension are critical for efficient migration.  
50 
 
51 
Significance 
52 
This study challenges the traditional view of Ras as solely a positive regulator of cell 
53 
functions by controlling of gene expression. Using optogenetics to rapidly modulate Ras 
54 
activity in Dictyostelium, we demonstrate a biphasic relationship between Ras activity 
55 
and migration: both excessive and insufficient Ras activity impair cell movement. 
56 
Importantly, these effects occur rapidly, independent of transcriptional changes, 
57 
revealing the mechanism by which Ras controls cell migration. The findings suggest 
58 
that optimal Ras activity and cortical tension are crucial for efficient migration, and that 
59 
targeting Ras in cancer therapy should consider the cell's initial state, aiming to push 
60 
Ras activity outside the optimal range for migration. This nuanced understanding of the 
61 
role of Ras in migration has significant implications for developing more effective cancer 
62 
treatments, as simply inhibiting Ras might inadvertently promote metastasis in certain 
63 
contexts.

Introduction 
66 
Ras small GTPases are highly versatile proteins that play pivotal roles in numerous 
67 
signaling events, regulating fundamental cell functions such as growth, survival, 
68 
differentiation, and migration (1–3). A delicate balance of Ras activity is crucial for 
69 
proper gene expression, cellular functions, and tissue development and dysregulation of 
70 
Ras can lead to developmental abnormalities and the onset of diseases such as cancer 
71 
(4–6). It is generally believed that Ras serves as a positive regulator of these functions, 
72 
and Ras has garnered enormous attention as a therapeutic target, with most research 
73 
focused on proliferation and differentiation (7–9). However, recent studies of cell 
74 
migration have shown that effects of modulating Ras activity depend largely on the 
75 
initial state of the cell.   Studies in rapidly moving cells such as Dictyostelium 
76 
discoideum and human neutrophils have shown that constitutively active Ras isoforms 
77 
induce more protrusion formation as might be expected but excess protrusions can lead 
78 
to cell spreading that precludes movement while dominant negative Ras isoforms or 
79 
expression of RasGAPs that limit protrusions can promote cell polarization and 
80 
migration (10, 11).  These findings highlight the complexity of Ras signaling in cell 
81 
migration and show that the relationship between Ras activity and cell migration is not 
82 
straightforward.  The counterintuitive outcomes must be considered when assessing 
83 
Ras as a target to treat cancer.  An inhibitor that limits growth may in fact promote 
84 
metastasis.  Therefore, it is important to obtain a full picture of how modulating Ras 
85 
activity affects cell migration, as well as other aspects, such as cell morphology, 
86 
macropinocytosis, and mechanical properties.   
87 
During growth factor stimulation, transcriptional and translational events are not 
88 
immediately observable whereas the triggering of Ras/Raf/MEK/ERK and 
89 
Ras/PI3K/mTOR signal transductions cascades are readily apparent.  Cells undergo 
90 
immediate morphological changes caused by dramatic cytoskeleton remodeling, leading 
91 
to cell spreading, ruffles, and protrusion formation (12–15).  Furthermore, 
92 
optogenetically activating or inhibiting Ras can rapidly create new protrusions and guide 
93 
cell migration or eliminate protrusions and halt migration, respectively, on timescales 
94 
much faster than those required for changes in gene expression (10, 11).  
95 
Mutations in the human NF1 gene, a RasGAP, cause the severe genetic disorder 
96 
neurofibromatosis type 1 (16, 17).  The defects are typically associated with increased 
97 
proliferation with little consideration of effects on cell morphology and dynamics (18, 
98 
19).  However, macropinocytosis is upregulated in Ras-driven tumors and the process 
99 
appears to be essential for driving accelerated growth. Interestingly, activation of Ras 
100 
isoforms is dynamically associated with macropinocytotic cups in axenic strains of the 
101 
social amoeba Dictyostelium (20). This upregulation of macropinocytosis in these 
102 
strains is caused by inactivation of a single NF1 homologue which leads to 
103 
hyperactivated Ras activity and underlies the massive increase in macropinocytosis.  
104

Surprisingly, cells with activated Ras do not grow dramatically faster, suggesting that G1 
105 
checkpoint control is not critical in these free-living amoebae. For these reasons, this 
106 
model organism offers the possibility of studying the effects of Ras activity modulation 
107 
on cell dynamics without complication from effects on proliferation. 
108 
Ras activity is regulated by guanine nucleotide exchange factors (RasGEFs) and 
109 
GTPase-activating proteins (RasGAPs) (21, 22). The Dictyostelium genome includes 
110 
about 25 genes encoding RasGEFs and 14 for RasGAPs (23). Among them, Aimless 
111 
and GefR can activate RasC and RasG, and their loss-of-function mutants have defects 
112 
in chemotaxis(24). GefB, GefM, GefU, and GefX are all reportedly important for random 
113 
migration and spontaneous signaling events (23). RasGAPs C2GAP1 and C2GAP2 
114 
interact with RasC and RasG and are essential for GPCR-mediated Ras signaling in 
115 
chemotaxis (25–27). NF1, NfaA and IqgC are RasGAPs that are mainly involved in the 
116 
regulation of macropinocytosis or phagocytosis (20, 28–30). Previously, we found that 
117 
overexpression or optogenetically recruitment of C2GAPB in vegetative Dictyostelium 
118 
unexpectedly polarized cells, albeit its inhibitory role on Ras activity (10).  Considering 
119 
the complexity and importance of Ras signaling on cell shape and migration, we aim to 
120 
systematically modulate Ras activity over a broad spectrum and further extend our 
121 
investigation to GefA (Aimless) and NGAP (C2GAP1). Through overexpression or 
122 
optogenetic manipulation, we sought to determine their effects on cell migration, cell 
123 
morphology, signal transduction, macropinocytosis, as well as the mechanical 
124 
properties of cells. Our evidence suggests a biphasic relationship between Ras activity 
125 
and cell migration. By dissecting this critical node in the signaling network, we provide 
126 
new insights into the coordinated regulation of cellular processes by Ras signaling 
127 
pathways. 
128 
 
129 
RESULTS 
130 
Exchange Factor GefA Alters Cell Morphology and Migration 
131 
Because Ras is thought to function as a positive regulator in cell migration and 
132 
chemotaxis, we first examined the immediate effects of increased Ras activity on 
133 
cellular protrusions and migration directions. To accomplish this aim, we designed an 
134 
optogenetic system to recruit SspB fused GefA protein from the cytosol to the cell 
135 
membrane anchored iLiD when blue light is presented(31–33). We hypothesized that 
136 
recruiting more GefA protein at the front of cells would enlarge the protrusions, while 
137 
recruiting it to the back would create new protrusions and reverse migration directions 
138 
(Fig. 1A). When we locally illuminated a protrusion, the recruitment led to an expansion 
139 
of the protrusion within 30 seconds and induced cells to shift the migration direction 
140 
(Fig. 1B). Next, we attempted to make the cell reverse direction by recruiting GefA to 
141 
the back.  This manipulation did induce protrusions, but because vegetative 
142

Dictyostelium cells are less polarized, the direction changes were inconclusive.  
143 
Therefore, we loaded cells into microchannel devices which enforce a defined direction. 
144 
As shown in Fig. 1C, when GefA-expressing cells moved in one direction, we locally 
145 
concentrated GefA at the trailing edge. In less than 3 minutes, the cell switched 
146 
direction. We were able to repeatedly change directions by alternately recruiting GefA at 
147 
different positions.   The movement of the edge of the cell lagged the recruitment of the 
148 
GefA by 20-60 sec (Movie S1). Compared to no light treatment where cells move to 
149 
either left or right, the recruitment of GefA consistently guided cells move toward the 
150 
side of GefA accumulation (Fig. 1D). Strikingly, local recruitment of GefA can induce 
151 
new protrusions formation and guide the cell migration in Gβ-null cells, which are 
152 
defective in most receptor-mediated signal transduction events and do not carry out 
153 
chemotaxis (Fig. S1A).    
154 
Given the obvious effects of increasing Ras activity on promoting cellular protrusions 
155 
and guiding cell migration direction, we hypothesized that overexpressing GefA would 
156 
promote cell migration. Suspecting that inhibitory effects of continuous expression of 
157 
active Ras proteins on cytokinesis selects for low expressors, we used an inducible 
158 
system to control GefA expression.  Axenic vegetative cells exhibit an amoeboid shape 
159 
and often form several protrusions simultaneously. They are less polarized then 
160 
developed cells, but nevertheless display moderate movement. However, upon the 
161 
overnight induction of GefA expression, the cells exhibited either a large, flat, pancake-
162 
like single global protrusion or thin, elongated morphologies with multiple opposing 
163 
protrusions, depending on the expression level of the GefA (Fig. S2C). In either case, a 
164 
major reduction in migration speed and macropinocytosis occurred (Fig. S2E, S2F). 
165 
Time-lapse videos of GefA-expressing cells revealed that both morphological categories 
166 
of cells barely move with almost no changes in cell shape.  The cells appeared “frozen” 
167 
over 10 minutes, compared to the control cells which displayed frequent shape changes 
168 
and movement.  The differences in migration are highlighted by the color-coded overlay 
169 
(Fig. 1E).  Because of the flattened and stretched out morphology, the average height of 
170 
the GefA-expressing cells was around half of the control (Fig. 1F), and the cell basal 
171 
area and flatness increased more than two-fold (Fig. 1G; Fig. S2A). Quantification of 
172 
cell migration speed showed that GefA-expressing cells on average migrated at 0.5-1 
173 
μm/min, while wild-type cells migrated at 6 μm/min (Fig. 1H). The average aspect ratio 
174 
of GefA-expressing cells and wild-type cells was around 1.5, while that of the elongated 
175 
fraction ranged from 2.5-4.5 (Fig. S2B). Because the GefA-expressing cells have two 
176 
different morphologies, we suspected that it could be the heterogeneous expression 
177 
differences. Consistently, as shown in Fig. S2C, among the GefA-expressing cells, the 
178 
higher expression levels of GefA have a larger basal area and smaller aspect ratio. 
179 
To confirm that this phenotype was indeed caused by GefA rather than a secondary 
180 
effect of its expression, we conducted global recruitment of GefA. We anticipated that

global recruitment would not only recapitulate the overexpression phenotype but also 
182 
produce a stronger effect due to its faster and more direct activation. As shown in Fig. 
183 
1I, global recruitment of GefA rapidly induced multiple protrusions and expanded the cell 
184 
in every direction, resulting in increased basal area and immobilization (Movie S2), as 
185 
quantified in Fig. 1J and 1K. The aspect ratio did not change significantly due to the 
186 
irregular, but symmetrical, shape of GefA-expressing cells before recruitment (Fig. 
187 
S2D). The color-coded overlay illustrates the changes in movement and cell shape 
188 
before and after GefA recruitment, showing that after recruitment, the cells expanded 
189 
with more protrusions in all directions, resulting in zero net movement (Fig. 1L). 
190 
Similarly, the global recruitment of GefA expanded the Gβ-null cells in all directions, 
191 
leading to an impairment in migration, and captured by color-coded images (Fig. S1B, 
192 
S1C). Taken together, overexpressing GefA or global recruitment of GefA that increased 
193 
Ras activity resulted in cell expansion and impaired cell movement, as summarized in 
194 
Fig. 1M.  
195 
Exchange Factor GefA Activates Signal Transduction and Cytoskeletal Activities 
196 
Since overexpression or recruitment of GefA led to remarkable changes in cell 
197 
morphology and alterations in migration, we investigated whether the phenotypes were 
198 
indeed caused by hyperactivated Ras activity. We compared Ras activity in cells with 
199 
and without GefA expression using fluorescence-labelled RBD as a biosensor. As 
200 
shown in Fig. 2A, control cells showed micron-sized, dynamic patches of RBD signals 
201 
that marked protrusions, typically lasting for 1 or 2 minutes. In contrast, GefA-
202 
expressing cells displayed much larger RBD patches on the membrane that remained 
203 
relatively static for about 10 minutes in this example. When normalized to the cell 
204 
perimeters, the lengths of the RBD patches are increased more than two-fold in the 
205 
GefA-expressing cells compared to controls (Fig. 2B). Occasionally, RBD waves were 
206 
observed in single GefA-expressing cells, propagating across the cell and creating a 
207 
broad protrusion as the wave swept to the edge (Fig. 2A).  Such waves of Ras activity 
208 
are observed in electroshock-fused giant cells but are rare in single control cells not 
209 
expressing GefA. To further assess whether GefA increases events downstream of Ras 
210 
activity, we used biosensor PHcrac and LimE to examine PIP3 and F-actin 
211 
polymerization activity. Normally, PHcrac and LimE mirror RBD activity, occurring with 
212 
protrusions, as shown in the controls (Fig. 2C). As expected, in GefA-expressing cells, 
213 
LimE mimicked the RBD signals and indicated significantly increased actin 
214 
polymerization along the cell periphery, which could account for their flattened 
215 
phenotype. However, we did not observe PHcrac labeling on GefA-expressing cell 
216 
membranes (Fig. 2D). Additionally, to confirm that GefA expression promotes actin 
217 
polymerization, we fixed cells and stained them with phalloidin, an F-actin binding 
218 
reagent. As shown in Fig. 2E, phalloidin mainly stained macropinocytotic cups in control 
219 
cells, but in GefA-expressing cells, it exhibited massive staining on the entire cell

membrane along with many diffuse, short filament structures in the cytosol. These 
221 
results together indicate that GefA expression increases Ras activity and promotes 
222 
more actin polymerization around the entire cell periphery, which likely explains the 
223 
decreased migration in GefA-expressing cells, as actin polymerization everywhere 
224 
prevents movement in a specific direction. 
225 
The strong actin polymerization caused by GefA overexpression was induced by 
226 
overnight incubation with doxycycline, but the optogenetic recruitable GefA can promote 
227 
cell expansion within minutes.  We were curious to see if the fast expansion was caused 
228 
by rapid signaling activity and actin polymerization downstream of Ras activation, 
229 
independent of transcriptional activities.  To address this question, we observed Ras 
230 
activity and its downstream signaling events, PIP3 accumulation and actin 
231 
polymerization directly when recruiting GefA to the cell cortex. Before recruitment, only 
232 
a single RBD patch appeared at protrusions. Within 15 seconds after GefA recruitment, 
233 
the entire membrane displayed RBD binding, indicating a major, global increase in Ras 
234 
activity. The cell began to expand, making broader protrusions, and the RBD signals 
235 
remained on the membrane throughout the recruitment period (Fig. 2F), even in 
236 
latrunculin A treated cells (Fig. 2G). Concurrently, the LimE biosensor indicated that 
237 
actin polymerization increased along the cell periphery, consistent with the area 
238 
expansion of the cell, as quantified in the line scan (Fig. 2H). Actin waves were 
239 
sometimes observed during this expansion, usually emerging from the center and 
240 
propagating to the edge (Movie S3). Simultaneously, PIP3 accumulation showed a 
241 
dynamic increase on the membrane. However, the PIP3 response lasted for 5 minutes 
242 
before subsiding for the remainder of the time (Fig. 2I).  The transient nature of the 
243 
PIP3 response likely explains why we did not observe obvious PIP3 patches on the 
244 
membrane in cells incubated overnight with doxycycline (Fig. 2D). 
245 
NGAP Alters Cell Morphology and Migration 
246 
Since Ras small GTPases are regulated by both GAPs and GEFs, we examined the 
247 
effects of overexpressing or recruiting RasGAPs. Previously, we found that 
248 
overexpression of a C2 domain-containing RasGAP, C2GAPB, inhibited Ras activity, but 
249 
when globally expressed, C2GAPB polarized cells and promoted migration(10). Building 
250 
on this observation, we turned our attention to another C2 domain-containing RasGAP 
251 
in Dictyostelium, NGAP, to investigate its role in cell migration(25). 
252 
Given the inhibitory effect of NGAP on Ras activity, we hypothesized that its local 
253 
recruitment to the leading-edge protrusion would induce protrusion retraction, while its 
254 
global recruitment would result either in inducing polarity as does C2GAP or whole-cell 
255 
contraction and cessation of migration (Fig. 3A).  To test this hypothesis, we illuminated 
256 
a small region of the cell with blue light, thereby concentrating SspB-fused NGAP in that 
257 
region. Within 30 seconds, the illuminated area of the cell rapidly contracted, causing

the cell to start to migrate away from the light source (Fig. 3B). Next, we placed cells in 
259 
microchannel devices. Consistent with the results from the 2D experiments, the 
260 
enrichment of SspB-fused NGAP at the cell front induced a polarity switch (Fig. 3C). 
261 
NGAP recruitment consistently pushed the cell to move away from the light (Fig. 3D).  
262 
In contrast to cells on 2D substrates, cells in confinement exhibit directed migration, 
263 
which enables us to alternate their polarity repeatedly using SspB-fused NGAP (Movie 
264 
S4).  
265 
We further investigated the effects of overexpressing NGAP on cell morphology and 
266 
migration. When NGAP expression was induced with doxycycline, cells formed tiny 
267 
protrusions and rounded up, as compared to the normal-sized pseudopods in control 
268 
cells with a clear front and back (Fig. 3E). To determine if these tiny protrusions could 
269 
mediate cell migration, we recorded time-lapsed videos with and without NGAP 
270 
expressions. While control cells migrated a significant distance over 16 minutes, NGAP-
271 
expressing cells hardly moved (Fig. 3E). The migration tracks were captured by color-
272 
coded overlay images. Quantification of average cell migration speed showed that with 
273 
NGAP expression, cell migration speed was 1.5 μm/min, compared to 5 μm/min for 
274 
control cells. NGAP-expressing cells also had a smaller aspect ratio but a similar basal 
275 
cell area and volume when compared to control cells, indicating they were rounder and 
276 
might not form large protrusions (Fig. 3F-H, Fig. S4A). Since NGAP expression reduces 
277 
cell migration speed, we examined the correlation between NGAP expression levels 
278 
and migration speed. As might be expected, we detected a negative correlation 
279 
between NGAP intensity and cell migration speed (Fig. S4B), further indicating that 
280 
NGAP inhibits cell migration. 
281 
Next, to directly examine the inhibitory role of NGAP on cell migration and protrusion 
282 
formation, we globally recruited SspB-fused NGAP to the entire cell cortex.  This 
283 
recruitment of NGAP caused a dramatic whole-cell contraction within a short time (Fig. 
284 
3I, Movie S5). Cell area also decreased more than two-fold after NGAP recruitment 
285 
(Fig. 3J).  The cells expressing the recruitable version of NGAP already had impaired 
286 
cell migration and rounded morphology before recruitment and their average migration 
287 
speed and aspect ratio did not change significantly before and after recruitment (Fig. 
288 
3K, 3L). The whole-cell contraction after NGAP recruitment was also shown in the color-
289 
coded overlay image (Fig. 3M). Based on the local, global recruitment and 
290 
overexpression results, NGAP appears to inhibit cellular protrusions by inducing 
291 
contraction, thereby halting cell migration. To further test this idea, we examined the 
292 
effects of NGAP recruitment in myosin heavy chain null cells and RacE null cells, both 
293 
of which have defects in contraction. As expected, neither local nor global recruitment of 
294 
NGAP caused significant changes in area in the null mutants, compared to the two-fold 
295 
changes in area reduction in wild-type (Fig. S3A-H; Fig. 3I).

NGAP Alters Signal Transduction and Cytoskeletal Activities 
297 
Given that NGAP is a RasGAP, we examined whether the phenotypes caused by its 
298 
expression or recruitment were due to decreased Ras activity. We first compared RBD 
299 
signals in cells with and without NGAP expression. Control cells exhibited broad, long-
300 
lasting RBD-labeled protrusions, whereas NGAP-expressing cells only had tiny and 
301 
transient RBD-labeled protrusions. The ratio of RBD patch to cell perimeter length 
302 
showed that Ras activity decreases with NGAP expression (Fig. 4A and 4B). Similarly, 
303 
we further assessed PIP3 activity and actin polymerization, which typically mirrors RBD 
304 
signal patterns.  As anticipated, PHcrac and LimE only transiently lit up on small puncta-
305 
like protrusions in NGAP-expressing cells, compared to multiple wide patches in control 
306 
cells, clearly demonstrating that NGAP plays an inhibitory role on Ras, PI3K, and actin 
307 
polymerization activities (Fig. 4C and 4D). Ras/PIP3 patches are also indicators of 
308 
macropinocytosis; uptake measurements revealed that macropinocytosis was 
309 
dramatically reduced upon NGAP expression (Fig. S4C, S4D). 
310 
Interestingly, among the NGAP-expressing population, some cells are flatter and 
311 
showed LimE ring patterns around the periphery close to the bottom surface. These 
312 
actin rings appeared to be “standing waves” that propagate slowly or did not propagate 
313 
relative to the cell (Fig. 4E). This unique ring structure of actin was also confirmed by 
314 
phalloidin staining in NGAP-expressing cells, which was distinct from the protrusions in 
315 
control cells (Fig. 4F). Both small LimE-labeled protrusions and non-propagating actin 
316 
rings could contribute to the decreased migration of NGAP-expressing cells.  
317 
We next tested whether NGAP functions as a RasGAP to inhibit Ras activity and its 
318 
downstream PI3K and actin polymerization activity, thereby slowing down cell migration. 
319 
To test this, we used our optogenetic system to globally recruit NGAP and monitored 
320 
RBD and LimE signals as readouts. Before recruitment, we detected tiny RBD and LimE 
321 
labeling on the small protrusions of NGAP-expressing cells. Upon blue light activation, 
322 
NGAP was recruited to the cell cortex within 30 seconds, rapidly diminishing RBD and 
323 
LimE signals, resulting in whole-cell contraction within 3 minutes, which was maintained 
324 
for at least 10 minutes (Fig. 4G, 4H, Movie S6). Altogether, these results indicate that 
325 
NGAP inhibits cell migration by decreasing Ras activity and actin polymerization, 
326 
leading to the elimination of protrusions and cell contraction. 
327 
Optimum Ras Activity and Cortical Tension Dictate Effective Migration 
328 
Expression of three Ras regulators, GefA, C2GAPB, and NGAP, cause either 
329 
spreading, tightly localized protrusions, or tiny unfocused protrusions, which either 
330 
halted migration, increased migration, or decreased migration, respectively.  The shape 
331 
and size of the protrusions also suggested that differences in mechanical properties, 
332 
such as cortical tension, may occur.

To measure their cortical tension, we performed micropipette aspiration in GefA-, 
334 
C2GAPB-, and NGAP-expressing cell lines and compared the values to those of wild 
335 
type cells(34, 35). We determined the length of extension into the pipette versus the cell 
336 
radius at various pressures and calculated the cortical tension (Fig. 5B). We observed 
337 
that NGAP- and C2GAPB-expressing cells had lower cortical tension than the control 
338 
cells, while GefA-expressing cells had increased cortical tension (Fig. 5C).  
339 
These initial cortical tension measurements were conducted on cell-lines that stably 
340 
express the Ras regulators.  In contrast, the signaling and morphological changes 
341 
observed in fast moving cells are much more rapid. To test whether Ras manipulation 
342 
has immediate effects on cortical tension, we leveraged our optogenetic system. 
343 
Because the light-induced recruitment occurred immediately and is reversible, we 
344 
decided to measure the cortical tension of the same cell twice. The first time was 
345 
performed without blue light illumination. After the cell reached the critical point, we 
346 
relaxed the cell and then measured the cortical tension again with blue light activation. 
347 
We hypothesized that acutely altered Ras activity would augment the change of cortical 
348 
tension, and as expected, recruitment of GefA further increased cortical tension by 
349 
about 1.7-fold while recruitment of NGAP decreased cortical tension ~40% from 0.8 
350 
nN/μm to 0.5 nN/μm (Fig. 5D-G). However, the recruitment of empty vector control had 
351 
little effect on the change of cortical tension before or after recruitment (Fig. 5J, 5K). 
352 
Because recruitment of C2GAPB increased myosin II contraction, it might lead to an 
353 
increase in the cortical tension. As shown in Fig. 5H and 5I, recruitment of C2GAPB 
354 
slightly increased the cortical tension, but not as significantly as the changes elicited by 
355 
recruiting GefA or NGAP, which could mean that C2GAPB is within the optimal range of 
356 
both Ras activity and cortical tension for most effective migration.    
357 
Discussion  
358 
In this study, we expanded our investigation into the role of Ras activity in regulating 
359 
cellular migration dynamics. Using Dictyostelium as a model, we demonstrated that 
360 
acute modulation of Ras activity via Ras GEFs and GAPs induces striking alterations in 
361 
cell shape and migration behavior. By employing optogenetic tools, we replicated 
362 
phenotypes previously observed in overexpression studies, but on significantly shorter 
363 
timescales, highlighting the immediate influence of Ras on cell motility. Furthermore, the 
364 
immediate effects of Ras modulation are independent of receptor inputs and 
365 
extracellular gradients, underscoring the self-autonomously organized properties of the 
366 
signaling network. Additionally, we examined how changes in Ras activity affect the 
367 
mechanical properties of the cell, revealing dynamic fluctuations in cortical tension that 
368 
correspond with variations in Ras signaling. Our findings establish a biphasic 
369 
relationship between Ras activity levels, cortical tension, and migration efficiency, 
370 
emphasizing the necessity of balanced Ras signaling and cortical tension for optimal 
371 
cellular motility.

Distinct outcomes were observed between local and global recruitment of RasGEF and 
373 
RasGAP. While local recruitment promoted or inhibited protrusions as anticipated, 
374 
global recruitment of these Ras modulators resulted in immobilized cell movement. We 
375 
propose that the amplification of Ras signaling by global recruitment pushes the system 
376 
to an extreme imbalance, which results in reduction in cell motility. In our previous work, 
377 
we identified an optimal level of Ras activity that maximizes cell migration (10). Building 
378 
on this, our current findings further suggest a biphasic relationship between Ras activity 
379 
and cell migration, where both insufficient and excessive Ras activity impair motility. 
380 
Furthermore, as cortical tension has been implicated in regulating cell shape and 
381 
migration behavior (36–39), we characterized the effects of our Ras modulators on 
382 
cortical tension. The results showed that cortical tension parallels change in Ras activity. 
383 
Together, these findings indicate the existence of a “Goldilocks”, optimal range of Ras 
384 
activity and cortical tension that regulates cell behavior, as demonstrated in this study 
385 
through its impact on migration. 
386 
On an unrestricted 2D glass surface, while local recruitment of RasGEF or RasGAP did 
387 
induce or inhibit protrusions, cells lacked sufficient polarity to support sustained 
388 
changes in cell migration. In contrast, within uncoated channels, cells migrated as 
389 
polarized cells even without any external stimulus. The channel setup also allowed for 
390 
more precise optogenetic manipulation of migration direction and behavior. In the 
391 
channels, it was clear that local enrichment of RasGEF at the trailing edge of migrating 
392 
cells in confinement was sufficient to reverse their migration directionality. Similarly, 
393 
optogentic recruitment of RasGAP, albeit at the leading edge of migrating cells, changed 
394 
migration direction. The effects of these distinct optogenetic stimulations on migration 
395 
directionality were evident in microchannels than on 2D because of the highly directed 
396 
migration induced by confining microchannel walls. Importantly, the accumulation of 
397 
RasGEF or RasGAP consistently preceded the directional changes of the cells. 
398 
Furthermore, even on 2D unrestricted surface, the global activation of Ras through 
399 
RasGEF recruitment overrode chemotactic stimuli in wild-type cells, and local activation 
400 
directly induced protrusions and polarity changes in Gβ-null cells. These findings 
401 
provide strong evidence that Ras activity provides a powerful signaling input necessary 
402 
for directed migration, functioning independently of receptor-mediated input. 
403 
Although Ras is generally believed to regulate cellular behavior and cytoskeletal 
404 
remodeling through long-term gene regulation (40–42), our previous work and this study 
405 
using optogenetics demonstrates that Ras controls cell migration over short time 
406 
periods(10, 11). Notably, both cell shape and migration behaviors changed dramatically 
407 
within seconds to minutes, accompanied by corresponding changes in cortical tension 
408 
as Ras activity was modulated.  Although cell state changes are often associated with 
409 
transcriptional regulation(43), our findings highlight the effects of short-term Ras activity 
410 
fluctuations that are independent of transcriptional changes.  Our results indicate that

413

414 
question of how these immediate, non-transcriptional effects integrate and coordinate 
415

Dictyostelium stable lines were generated by electroporating 2 µg DNA into 107 cells

using a chilled 0.1-cm cuvette (Bio-Rad, cat. no. 1652089) at 0.85 kV/25 µF twice with a

Vegetative Dictyostelium were adhered on an eight-well coverslip chamber for 30 min.

supported with ZEN Blue software. All images were acquired with either 63×/1.40

globally to activate recruitment. Image acquisition and photoactivation were performed

Microfluidic devices composed of polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) with an array of parallel

Image Analysis 
562 
All images were analysed with Fiji/ImageJ 1.52i (NIH), MATLAB (Mathworks), and 
563 
Python v.3.10. We utilized GraphPad Prism 8 (GraphPad), MATLAB (Mathworks), and 
564 
Microsoft Excel (Microsoft) for plotting our results. 
565 
 
566 
To segment the cells, images were first binarized using ImageJ, by adjusting threshold 
567 
to cover all pixels of the wave or cell. The range was not reset and the ‘Calculate 
568 
threshold for each image’ option was unchecked. Subsequently, using the ‘Analyze 
569 
Particle’ function, a size-based thresholding was applied (to exclude non-cell particles) 
570 
and cell masks were generated. Next, ‘Fill holes’, ‘Erode’ and ‘Dilate’ options were 
571 
applied, sequentially and judiciously, to obtain the proper binarized mask for cells. The 
572 
segmented cells were used for cell outline overlays. For RBD patches quantification, the 
573 
length of RBD patches and the total cell perimeter were segmented with Fiji line tool 
574 
and calculated the ratio. Cell speed, area and aspect ratio quantifications were 
575 
described previously(10). Macropinocytosis uptake was analyzed by outlining each cell 
576 
and quantifying the total FITC fluorescence signal within each outline divided by the cell 
577 
area. Data for Fig. 1D and 3D were obtained by custom code in MATLAB.  Cells were 
578 
segmented using thresholding, followed by filling holes (imfill) and morphological closing 
579 
(imclose). The ‘regionprops’ command was used to find the cell centroid. The centroid of 
580 
the laser was obtained similarly.  Instantaneous speeds were obtained by one step 
581 
differences and separated depending on whether a laser was present or whether it was 
582 
to the left or right of the cell. Statistical test was done using a Mann-Whitney test in 
583 
MATLAB.
